# Transcrição: 03-Mastering Your Delivery

- **Fonte:** 03-Mastering Your Delivery
- **Duração:** 09:36
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:07]

Confidence is critical, ineffective communication. We must master our delivery to be effective with our audiences. It's a very simple equation. Confidence equals competence. The more confident you are, the more competent and credible your audience will see you as. Now of course you can take this to an extreme and I'm not saying be overconfident and arrogant. But we have to work on our confidence to be effective. So let's talk about the biggest barrier to confidence and that's anxiety. Most people get nervous speaking in high-stakes situations. In fact, up to 85% of people report getting nervous when they're up in front of others. And quite frankly, I think the other 15% are lying. We need to focus our communication and our practice to make sure that we come off as confident. In order to do that, we have to focus on two things. We have to focus on the symptoms and the sources. And once we've got that managed, we'll talk about ways to actually enhance our delivery. So we manage the anxiety so we're confident and then we put in some kickers to actually enhance our delivery. When it comes to sources of anxiety, these are the things that initiate and exacerbate our anxiety. The things that start us feeling nervous and make it worse. The biggest thing that gets in our way is our desire to do it right. When we communicate, we want to communicate perfectly. We want to give the best answers. We want to give the most useful information. And this puts a tremendous amount of pressure on ourselves. It boils down to simple cognitive load. Your brain in many ways is like a computer. It's not a perfect analogy, but here it works. If you have lots of windows open on a laptop or lots of apps running on your phone, every one of those apps and windows is functioning less well because the others are open. Your brain works exactly the same way. If I am constantly judging and evaluating what I'm saying, that means I only have partial bandwidth to focus on what I am saying. So I like to say focus on connection, not perfection. There is no right way to communicate. There are better ways and worse ways, but no one right way. So let's not worry about doing it the best we can, and instead let's focus our energy on connecting to our audience. So think about how what you're saying will help your audience. By being audience-centric, the spotlight is no longer on you. It's on the value you're bringing for them. What I do before I ever speak is I will always say to myself, I have value to bring. By saying that positive affirmation in mantra, it reminds me to focus on the needs of my audience. I also turn my attention to how they will benefit, and sometimes I even directly ask them questions

## [03:07 - 06:08]

so I can help them see the value that they're getting. So one big source of anxiety is striving for perfection, and we get around that by focusing on our audience. It's all about connection, not perfection. The second source of anxiety I'd like to cover has to do with our goal. I've talked about in our previous sessions the importance of having a goal, but unfortunately, sometimes our goal can make us more nervous. A goal is a future state. My students want to get a good grade. The entrepreneurs I coach want to get funding or expand their businesses. You might want your audience to feel compelled by what you're saying. These are all future states, and what makes us nervous is our concern of not achieving that goal. In other words, our anxiety is brought about by a potential negative future outcome. So the best way to manage that is to become present-oriented. If you're in the moment by definition, you're not worried about the future. So how do I get present-oriented? There are lots of things you can do. Do something physical, some light exercise, walk around the building. Do what athletes do and listen to a song or a playlist. I know a professional speaker who actually plays Tetris before she gets up on stage. You can do something like I do, which is to say tongue twisters before I start. By saying a tongue twister, I warm up my voice and I get present-oriented because you can't say a tongue twister right without being in the present moment. So we can short-circuit our fear that comes from not achieving our goal by being really present-oriented. There are other sources of anxiety as well, but these are the two big ones. Focus on connection over perfection, be in the present moment. That's one side of the confidence equation, sources. But we also have to think about symptoms. What goes on for us physically and mentally when we get anxious? If you're like most people, your heart beats faster, you get a little shaky, you might say a lot of ums and uhs. This is normal and natural. Your body is responding to the threat of being on the spot and speaking in the moment. There are things we can do to reduce that. First, we can take some deep belly breaths. The kind of breath you would take if you've ever done yoga or tai chi or chi gong where you really fill your lower abdomen. The key to the success of this type of breathing is the exhale. You want your exhale to be twice as long as your inhale. So if you take a three count in, take a six count out. It is during the exhalation that all of the relaxation happens. You only need to do this a few times to feel better. For those of you that get shaky, that's adrenaline running through your body. Drenaline's job is to move you from threat to safety.

## [06:08 - 09:09]

So involve movement. Do gestures. If you're standing, you can actually move forward as you address your audience or side to side as you transition. This movement allows the anxiety to dissipate. For those of you that use filler words, ums, uhs, likes, and I mean. These are the result of your brain trying to think quickly and your body not following along. We can manage this first and foremost by realizing the goal is not to eliminate them. It is normal and natural to have some filler words. What we want to do is reduce the frequency so they're not distracting. How do we do that? We practice how we end our sentences. The filler words that are most distracting are the ones that come in silence between our ideas. And then we start speaking some more. If you practice ending your sentences so you are completely out of breath, you must inhale before you can say something else. When we inhale, we cannot speak. Speaking is an exit only event. Air has to come out. So if I train myself to end my sentences completely out of breath, I take that inhale, preventing me from saying a filler word, but also building in a pause. Pauses are good. So there are things we can do to manage the symptoms of anxiety, deep breathing, movement that is appropriate, and watching how we end our sentences. In so doing, we begin to appear more confident. Now there's some things we can do to actually catalyze our communication as well. When we speak, we want to make sure that our voice is varied, both in rate and volume. Many of us, when we get nervous, restrict our vocal range. So a great way to improve this is to add emotive words, adjectives and adverbs. I would never say, I'm really excited to be here. No, I'd say I'm really excited to be here. Those emotive words, really excited, invite that vocal variation. So I challenge you to find ways of adding emotive words to help. The last thing I'd like to mention that will help you engage your audience is to change your perspective. Instead of being in the role of pushing information out, broadcasting, think of it as a conversation. If you can set yourself up to ask questions of your audience, it will really help you to engage and change your demeanor. Many of us are much more comfortable being in conversation than in presentation. So when I actually practice my presentations and practice is critical, I craft my narrative in terms of questions. So I might say, today I'd like to answer four questions for you. Question number one is this. And then I imagine I'm asking myself that question and I answer it.

## [09:09 - 09:36]

So I become more conversational. I might actually ask you questions as well and then answer the questions and responses that you have for me. So there are things we can do to turbocharge our delivery in terms of confidence, in terms of vocal variation, and in terms of being more conversational. By putting these into practice, you will not only engage your audience better, but you will feel more confident and compelling.
